title: Beyond the Biotech Race — Right-Sizing U.S.-China Competition in a Human-Centered Industry
text:
Key Takeaways

U.S.-China biotech competition is often framed as a linear, zero-sum race at the technological frontier. A more accurate framework is asymmetric convergence. Biotechnology is not a single chokepoint technology like semiconductors but rather constitutes a multi-layered biomedical ecosystem spanning basic science, translational research, clinical trials, manufacturing, regulatory review, commercialization, data governance, and healthcare delivery. The United States and China are increasingly competing across the same ecosystem, but with different strengths at different layers of the value chain.The United States retains enduring structural advantages, but they require renewal. U.S. leadership remains strongest in foundational science, first-in-class discovery, academic medical centers, venture-backed translational research, deep capital markets, FDA credibility, global standard-setting, and the ability to attract and retain world-class scientific talent. These advantages have long made the United States the orchestrator of frontier biomedical innovation. But they are not self-sustaining. Funding instability, pressure on universities, uncertainty around regulatory capacity, and disruptive and overly restrictive immigration policies could weaken the talent and discovery engine that has historically powered American biotech leadership.China’s biotech rise is no longer simply a story of catch-up or imitation. China has become a systemic peer competitor in several operational domains, including research scale, clinical trial execution, biological manufacturing, asset formation, regional biotech clustering, and global out-licensing. Its strengths are most pronounced where biotechnology functions as an industrial and engineering system: scaling research output, executing trials quickly, mobilizing capital, integrating hospitals and manufacturing, and generating licensable assets at speed.China’s convergence is structural and likely to outlast any fixed policy window. Beijing’s progress is not merely the result of temporary subsidies or isolated breakthroughs. It reflects years of institutional adaptation, talent recruitment, regulatory reform, capital mobilization, data mercantilism, IP capture, and participation in global biomedical networks. Because these capabilities are embedded across multiple layers of the biotech value chain, they are unlikely to be reversed by any single policy tool or short-term action.Biotech should not be treated as exactly analogous to semiconductors. Export controls and “protect” and “restrict” measures may be necessary in specific areas involving sensitive data, dual-use capabilities, supply-chain vulnerabilities, or modalities of national security concern. But broad, undifferentiated restrictions in biotech risk weakening U.S. competitiveness, reducing visibility into global biomedical developments, disrupting supply chains, and slowing innovation in areas where patient outcomes are directly at stake.The United States needs an affirmative, layer-specific strategy. A more effective approach should distinguish among the different layers of the biotech ecosystem: preserve openness in basic science where it underpins U.S. leadership; apply targeted guardrails where national security risks are real; strengthen domestic capacity in biomanufacturing, translational research, and supply-chain resilience; lead in regulatory and data-governance standards; and maintain U.S. influence in global biomedical networks.Competition with China should be used as a catalyst to upgrade the U.S. biotech system. The central U.S. challenge is not only how to restrict China where risks are genuine, but how to compete from a position of renewed strength. This means reinvigorating NIH and NSF funding for basic science, improving translational efficiency, expanding biomanufacturing capacity, supporting clinical trial modernization, strengthening regulatory agility, and preserving America’s ability to attract global talent.Health and medicine remain among the few domains where bounded, de-risked cooperation is still strategically viable. Shared disease burdens, asymmetric capabilities, patient needs, and the humanitarian nature of biomedical innovation create conditions for narrow cooperation that few other sectors can match. Cancer, rare diseases, mental health, chronic illness, aging, pandemic preparedness, antimicrobial resistance, and ethical governance are systemic challenges that do not stop at national borders.A realistic cooperation agenda should be narrow, practical, and subject to clear guardrails. Potential areas include disease-specific clinical trial corridors, rare disease research, oncology pilots, mental health as a shared public health challenge, affordability and access, regulatory learning, and practical training in healthcare delivery and regulatory science. These channels should avoid unrestricted data sharing or sensitive technology transfer and instead focus on privacy protection standards, patient safety, implementation capacity, and measurable health outcomes.The central policy question is not whether the United States should compete with China in biotechnology. It must. The question is how to right-size that competition. A sound strategy should protect national security where risks are real, preserve U.S. scientific and industrial leadership where advantages remain decisive, invest in domestic capacity where vulnerabilities exist, and sustain carefully bounded cooperation where it serves American interests and improves human health.

I. Introduction

For decades, U.S. policymakers and industry leaders have interpreted China’s ambitions in biotechnology through a simple heuristic: that China’s biotech sector trails that of the United States by a margin sufficient to sustain U.S. dominance, and that enduring U.S. leadership was an advantage taken for granted. This framing has now become analytically misleading—and strategically misguided.Recent assessments have sparked a sharpened sense of urgency. An April 2025 report by the congressionally mandated National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology (NSCEB) warns that the United States has a “three-year window” to preserve its leadership or risk ceding strategic advantage to China. In December 2025, the Commission released a follow-up report asserting that this window is closing far faster than anticipated. These warnings rightly sound the alarm—particularly in highlighting the size and scale of China’s advancement and offering candid assessments of the breathtaking speed at which competitive dynamics are shifting.At the same time, a singularly race-centered framing rests on an incomplete assumption: that biotechnology competition is one single, linear contest in which one country leads, another catches up, and the policy task is simply to preserve distance. Biotechnology is far more complex. It is not one technology but a compendium of technologies and capabilities—spanning mRNA platforms, antibodies, cell therapies, gene editing, DNA sequencing and synthesis, cryo-electron microscopy, biological manufacturing, clinical trial systems, and regulatory pathways. Unlike semiconductors or AI models, biotechnological capabilities cannot be captured by one or two decisive parameters alone. Nor does biotech innovation move through a simple linear process. It occurs across a multi-layered ecosystem: basic science, translational research, clinical infrastructure, patient recruitment, manufacturing scale, regulatory review, commercialization, reimbursement, and healthcare delivery. Distinct from many other strategic technologies, biotechnology is also embedded within national healthcare systems, involving patients, hospitals, payers, providers, regulators, and public trust.This structure of the innovation value chain is the core reason U.S.-China biotech competition is better understood as asymmetric convergence rather than a linear race. Biotechnology does not have a single decisive chokepoint equivalent to advanced lithography in semiconductors. Value is created across a distributed chain comprising basic discovery, translational platforms, clinical trials, patient recruitment, data systems, manufacturing, regulatory approval, commercialization, and healthcare delivery. A country can lag in one layer while gaining strength in another. It can depend on another country in one layer and maintain leverage over another. This makes biotechnology’s progress difficult to contain, slow, or reverse through blunt, export control-style tools, and instead requires a more differentiated policy framework to be effective.Thus, this paper instead proposes a framework of asymmetric convergence to understand the competitive dynamics playing out between the United States and China. China is neither uniformly behind nor simply catching up to the United States but is progressing unevenly across the biotechnology value chain. More importantly, the forces driving this convergence are structural and self-reinforcing, built through years of institutional learning and deeply embedded in the global innovation ecosystem, as well as the product of complex and interdependent value chains and development pipelines that, unlike other sectors, are not amenable to simple strategies of decoupling or reversal.   Oversimplifying this shift risks serious policy consequences. As CCA colleagues Brendan Kelly and Ambassador Craig Allen argue in their recent analysis, growing U.S. dependence on Chinese active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) for drug supply chains, or on Chinese contract development and manufacturing organizations (CDMOs) for biomanufacturing, has already engendered structural vulnerabilities that pose security concerns. But with patient health outcomes at stake, unmanaged bifurcation and poorly planned decoupling—even in a laudable effort to rectify these problems—would impose costs on medical innovation and patient outcomes. To be sure, these dependencies in biotech are legitimate concerns that deserve serious scrutiny—particularly supply-chain chokepoints, dual-use vulnerabilities, control of biological data, and putative ties of Chinese CDMOs and biotech firms to the military, as recent congressional attention has highlighted. This paper does not argue that those concerns should be dismissed. On the contrary, it argues that those concerns should be addressed with more precise and powerful tools—because failing to do so would incur significant negative externalities.It is also the fundamentally human nature of this sector that leads to the paper's second core argument: beyond the biotech race, medicine and health may emerge as one of the few remaining stabilizers in a U.S.-China relationship increasingly shaped by distrust. To that end, this paper also advances a clear-eyed and realistic agenda for selective, derisked cooperation, built around specific diseases, concrete safeguards to address security concerns, and demonstrable benefits for patients and human life.

II. A Layered Framework to Evaluate Biotech Competitiveness

To assess China’s relative position versus that of the United States, we must understand the various stages in developing a drug: basic science and discovery; preclinical and translational research; clinical trial infrastructure; manufacturing and scale; and the regulatory and commercialization ecosystem. Each layer reflects a distinct set of capabilities, institutional arrangements, and competitive dynamics. Within this framework, the United States and China occupy different structural positions.

The United States: Leadership through Renewal

Critically, despite China’s strength, the United States retains several enduring structural advantages that anchor its leadership in global biotechnology.First, the United States boasts an unparalleled basic science ecosystem and university research enterprise that constitute the crown jewel of the nation. Decades of sustained government investment in universities and academic research, dating back to the postwar era, have created a deep reservoir of foundational knowledge. This system has been supported by a federal commitment to open inquiry and long-term exploratory research, most notably through the National Institutes of Health (NIH), the world’s largest biomedical research funder, and the National Science Foundation (NSF). Rooted in the vision articulated by Vannevar Bush in Science, the Endless Frontier—that sustained public investment in basic science is the foundation of future technological and medical advances—this ecosystem continues to generate paradigm-shifting discoveries and scientific knowledge that underpin future therapeutic breakthroughs and give rise to new modalities. It is therefore no coincidence that many of the defining discoveries of the 1950s and 1960s that ushered in the age of molecular biology—uncovering mechanisms of DNA replication and regulation, deciphering the genetic code, elucidating the means by which genetic information is transmitted—unfolded across America’s leading research institutions.Second, America leads in first-in-class discovery and frontier platform technologies in certain key areas. From mRNA vaccines to CRISPR-based gene editing to immunotherapy and genomic medicine, many of the defining advances in modern molecular biology and biotechnology originate within U.S.-based institutions and firms. These innovations shape the direction of the entire field and serve as the foundational platforms upon which therapeutic modalities are built. Third, the American biotech sector benefits from a highly sophisticated venture capital and financial ecosystem. U.S. capital markets have long played a vital role in de-risking biopharmaceutical assets as they ascend the value chain. Venture financing enables rapid translation of academic discoveries into companies and the transition of smaller biotech firms into substantial growth mode. Deep life sciences expertise among investors, combined with robust exit pathways through IPOs and acquisitions, enables rapid scaling of early-stage companies and efficient allocation of capital. Fourth, the United States maintains global regulatory leadership through the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). FDA standards remain a global benchmark not only for novel biologics and small-molecule drugs, but also across a broader landscape that includes cell and gene therapies, diagnostics, medical devices, clinical trial design, manufacturing quality, labeling, and post-market surveillance. For companies seeking global commercialization, the ability to navigate the U.S. regulatory pathway often confers a significant advantage, as FDA approval continues to carry exceptional credibility with regulators, clinicians, investors, and patients around the world. To be sure, the American regulatory regime has long faced criticism for inefficiency and other areas in need of reform. Yet the FDA’s rigorous focus on safety, evidence, and strict approval standards remains one of the core reasons the U.S. biomedical pipeline is trusted globally.Taken together, these longstanding strengths fundamentally position the United States as the orchestrator of frontier innovation—defining technological trajectories, unveiling novel modalities and platforms, birthing leading companies, and setting global standards.This leadership, however, is neither guaranteed nor permanent. The same structural advantages that have underpinned U.S. biotechnology dominance require continual renewal: sustained public investment, world-class universities, trusted regulatory institutions, deep capital markets, and above all, the ability to attract, train, and retain the world’s best scientific talent.Recent upheavals in the American biomedical research system—including proposed reductions in NIH and NSF funding, shifting federal R&D priorities, political pressure on leading universities, uncertainty surrounding FDA capacity and standards, and increasingly restrictive immigration debates—risk weakening these foundations. The talent dimension is particularly important. American leadership has never depended solely on domestic capacity. Rather, it has been built through extraordinary openness to global talent and scientific exchange. International students, postdoctoral researchers, physician-scientists, entrepreneurs, and senior investigators have powered the U.S. discovery engine, staffed laboratories, founded companies, led clinical programs, and connected American institutions to the frontiers of global science.If the United States becomes a less attractive or less predictable destination for these individuals, the effects will compound over time. Fewer top students will enter American graduate programs; fewer scientists will remain to build laboratories and companies; fewer discoveries will move from academic insight to commercial translation; and more talent may relocate to Canada, Europe, Singapore, China, and other emerging innovation hubs. The risk is not merely short-term disruption, but gradual erosion of the American biotechnology engine itself: public funding supports universities; universities attract talent; talent generates discovery; discovery feeds startups and pharmaceutical pipelines; capital scales innovation; and FDA credibility enables global commercialization. If these links weaken simultaneously, the United States could gradually lose the strengths that have historically made it the global leader in biomedical innovation.

China’s Rise Across the Biotechnology Ecosystem

If the United States remains the world’s leading discovery ecosystem, China has emerged as its most significant biotechnology challenger. Its rise can no longer be adequately described as merely “catching up” or copying innovation developed elsewhere. Over the past decade—and especially during the last five years—it has evolved from a fast follower into a systemic peer competitor. While China still trails the United States in several foundational dimensions, it now leads in important operational domains, particularly research scale, clinical trial execution, manufacturing capacity, and commercialization speed.

Science and Research Capacity

One of the most visible shifts has occurred in scientific research. By some measures, China has overtaken the United States in the volume of high-quality scientific output, reflecting not only scale but also decades of sustained investment in universities, research institutes, and national R&D programs. According to the 2025 Nature Index, eight of the world’s ten leading research institutions are now Chinese, with Harvard the only American institution remaining in the top ten. This development reflects a broader rebalancing in the geography of global science, driven by long-term policy support for basic research, aggressive recruitment of globally trained scientists, and the steady expansion of domestic research infrastructure.China’s rise is also supported by a major expansion in R&D investment. Measured in purchasing power parity terms, China has surpassed the United States in total R&D expenditure, an inflection point that would have been difficult to imagine a decade ago. In 2025, China’s research spending reached approximately $569 billion, continuing a trajectory of sustained high single-digit annual growth. Spending on basic research has also increased, crossing 7% of total R&D for the first time. To be sure, basic research still accounts for a smaller share of China’s total R&D spending than in the United States, where decades of NIH funding have created a deep and self-renewing pipeline of foundational discovery. But the priorities contained within China’s latest Five-Year Plan make clear that Beijing intends to continue increasing the share, quality, and strategic relevance of basic research.Publication volume, however, should not be confused with equivalent scientific impact. Much of China’s strength remains concentrated in engineering, optimization, scale-up, and application rather than in generating entirely new scientific paradigms, foundational biological discoveries, or novel research tools. Publication counts are also imperfect measures of originality, influence, reproducibility, and translational significance. These caveats matter.Yet they should not obscure the broader trend. China has become a major producer of science and is steadily building the institutional depth required to support a more ambitious biotechnology sector. This pattern is increasingly visible in health sciences as well. Although China still trails the United States in the Nature Index’s health sciences category, its share of high-impact biomedical research is rising rapidly. The direction of travel is clear: China is constructing the scientific base, institutional density, and research infrastructure necessary to support long-term biotechnology leadership.

Talent and Human Capital

Talent is central to China’s biotechnology transition. China now produces a vast pipeline of scientists and engineers, particularly in chemistry, chemical engineering, process development, manufacturing, and, increasingly, computational biology. These capabilities provide growing advantages in the applied and translational stages of biomedical innovation, where speed, iteration, execution, and scale are critical. China may not yet match the United States in Nobel-level biological discovery, elite physician-scientist networks, or venture-backed academic translation, but it is rapidly building a workforce capable of moving assets through the development pipeline quickly and at scale.This talent dynamic is also being reshaped by global mobility. A substantial share of U.S. biomedical innovation has long depended on foreign-born scientists, including Chinese-born graduate students, postdoctoral researchers, principal investigators, entrepreneurs, and industry scientists. They have helped staff university laboratories, found biotech companies, lead pharmaceutical R&D programs, and connect American institutions to global scientific networks. China has long sought to reverse this flow through active recruitment campaigns aimed at overseas scientific talent, including ethnic Chinese researchers. These efforts have been the focus of U.S. national security scrutiny in the past decade—and the FBI has described China’s talent recruitment programs as often involving undisclosed and illegal transfers of information, technology, and IP and directed in part by the United Front Work Department. As the U.S. research environment becomes more uncertain and is perceived to be subject to political turbulence, however, some of the international scientific talent in the U.S. is increasingly relocating to Europe or, in the case of Chinese-born scientists, returning to China, due to concerns about U.S. political headwinds, further strengthening China’s innovation ecosystem while weakening one of the United States’ historic advantages. 

Clinical Development, Commercialization, and Industrial Scale

If China’s gains in science and talent remain a work in progress, its transformation in clinical development has been decisive.Since 2021, China has emerged as the leading global location for clinical trials. At the regulatory level, the introduction of implied approval systems has reduced innovative drug review to 60 working days, and for some qualifying innovative drugs to 30 working days under 2025 reforms. The expanded use of investigator-initiated trials (IIT), which are overseen by the National Health Commission as opposed to the National Medical Products Administration and require only local hospital-level approval, has further accelerated early-stage proof-of-concept development by bypassing the additional regulatory constraints that apply to industry-sponsored trials. Once trials are underway, China’s large public hospitals concentrate patient populations at a scale and density that fragmented Western site networks cannot match, which enables significantly faster recruitment with a lower and more stable cost structure. Together, they have reshaped the economics of drug development, positioning China as a global hub for clinical execution.The consequences are increasingly visible across the global drug pipeline. Chinese firms now account for roughly 30% of experimental drugs under development worldwide and more than a quarter of global pharmaceutical licensing transactions. In 2025 alone, Chinese biotechnology companies completed more than 150 out-licensing deals with a combined value exceeding $130 billion. China is no longer simply adapting or manufacturing therapies developed elsewhere. It is increasingly generating assets that global pharmaceutical companies actively seek to acquire, license, or co-develop. In therapeutic areas such as antibody-drug conjugates, GLP-1 therapies, and CAR-T treatments, Chinese firms are increasingly competing at development speeds comparable to—and in some cases exceeding—those of Western counterparts.Underlying these developments is a broader structural shift in how China organizes innovation. The country’s emphasis on building an integrated innovation ecosystem—linking research, clinical development, and manufacturing within coordinated regional clusters, including, notably, Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park in Shanghai and Suzhou BioBAY—has accelerated what is traditionally a prolonged drug development cycle. By co-locating research institutions, hospitals, regulatory authorities, firms, and production facilities, China has created an environment in which scientific advances can move more rapidly from laboratory to market—while remaining within close physical proximity. This ecosystem is also reinforced by industrial policy tools—including subsidies, tax incentives, preferential financing, and procurement support—often deployed aggressively by provincial and local governments competing to build biotech hubs. This integration is reinforced by policy alignment across financing, regulation, and industrial development, enabling a degree of whole-of-nation coordination that is difficult to replicate elsewhere. These examples make the co-location logic concrete: hospitals, labs, investors, regulatory support, and manufacturing capacity sit close enough to shorten feedback loops and support China’s out-licensing momentum. 

AI and the Next Frontier

A further factor reshaping the competitive landscape is the growing convergence of artificial intelligence and biotechnology. AI is increasingly integrated across the drug development process—from target identification and molecule design to clinical trial recruitment, biomedical data analysis, and manufacturing optimization.Whether this transformation ultimately favors the United States or China remains unclear. The United States retains advantages in frontier AI models, computational infrastructure, cloud computing, and many of the world’s leading AI companies. China, meanwhile, brings complementary strengths in clinical data generation, trial execution, manufacturing scale, and biomedical deployment. As AI becomes more deeply embedded within biotechnology, success will depend less on leadership in either field individually and more on the ability to integrate advances across both.

China’s Remaining Constraints

Yet these advances coexist with important institutional limitations, including limitations on the freedom of scientific inquiry and exploration needed for frontier breakthroughs, problems with the structure of research incentives, and legal and political constraints that limit the full integration of state-funded university labs with global venture capital systems.And despite an abundance of STEM graduates, China also faces challenges in talent composition, particularly in the relative scarcity of physician-scientists who can bridge the gap between laboratory research and clinical application—for example, by turning bedside observations about why treatments fail into better trial design, biomarkers, and patient selection. Chinese hospitals, whose operational incentives prioritize patient throughput over research productivity, have historically underinvested in the protected time and institutional infrastructure that physician-scientist careers require. This type of interdisciplinary expertise is critical for translating scientific insights into effective therapies and remains a comparative strength of the U.S. system. As an example, while Tsinghua University is a leading research university in the sciences and engineering, its hospital ecosystem and physician-scientist community remain fragmented and relatively de-centralized, impeding the seamless progression from basic science to commercialization to clinical translation that large academic medical centers in the United States facilitate with ease. 

Asymmetric Convergence Across a Distributed Value Chain

These dynamics point to a conclusion more nuanced than either triumphalist or alarmist narratives allow.China’s strengths are most pronounced where biotechnology functions as an industrial and engineering system: scaling research output, executing clinical trials, manufacturing biologics, integrating industrial clusters, and generating licensable assets at speed.U.S. strengths are most pronounced where biotechnology functions as a discovery and translational ecosystem: advancing foundational science, generating first-in-class breakthroughs, integrating physician-scientists and academic medicine, attracting global talent, and converting discovery into globally trusted innovation.The result is not a simple race between two identical systems, nor a linear contest with a single winner. It is asymmetric convergence across a distributed value chain, with each country gaining strength at different layers of the biotechnology ecosystem.This distinction matters because it suggests that effective policy must also be layered: protecting sensitive capabilities where risks are real, renewing U.S. strengths where advantages must be maintained, preserving influence over global standards, and sustaining carefully bounded cooperation where doing so advances both U.S. interests and patient outcomes.

III. Understanding Asymmetric Convergence

The convergence between the United States and China in biotechnology is not driven by Chinese policy ambition alone. It is rooted first in the structure of the industry itself. Biotechnology is not a single technology with one decisive chokepoint; it is a multi-layered ecosystem spanning basic science, translational research, clinical infrastructure, patient recruitment, manufacturing, regulatory review, commercialization, data governance, and healthcare delivery. Capabilities are distributed unevenly across these layers, and leadership in one domain does not automatically translate into leadership across the entire system.This structure creates the conditions for asymmetric convergence. China does not need to replicate the full U.S. biomedical innovation model to become a serious competitor. It can remain weaker in open-ended foundational discovery while gaining strength in applied research, clinical execution, manufacturing scale, regulatory adaptation, asset formation, and commercialization. Conversely, the United States can retain decisive advantages in basic science, frontier discovery, academic medicine, venture-backed translation, and FDA credibility while facing growing competition in downstream and operational domains. This is the essence of asymmetric convergence: China does not need to become the United States to challenge the United States.This is why biotechnology competition cannot be understood as a simple linear race. In semiconductors, a small number of chokepoints can shape the competitive landscape. In biotechnology, value is created across a longer and more distributed development chain, much of it embedded in hospitals, patients, regulators, manufacturing systems, and pharmaceutical markets. As a result, convergence is more difficult to halt through containment alone and more likely to persist across political and policy cycles.China’s rise is therefore not simply the result of greater spending or industrial policy support. It reflects a broader process of institutional learning and selective adaptation across multiple layers of the biotechnology ecosystem. Beijing has not merely increased investment in biotechnology; it has selectively absorbed practices from the United States and other advanced biomedical systems and embedded them within its own policy, regulatory, clinical, and commercial architecture.China has also invested in new research institutions designed to support more inquiry-driven science and reduce reliance on incremental innovation alone. Institutions such as Westlake University reflect efforts to create research environments modeled in part on leading global universities. These initiatives do not yet replicate the depth, openness, or cumulative strengths of the U.S. university system, but they illustrate China’s ambition to move further upstream in the innovation process.Several reinforcing mechanisms have accelerated this convergence.

Talent and Knowledge Transfer

Talent has played a central role in China’s biotechnology development, though it should be understood as one mechanism of convergence rather than the whole story. Much as the United States built its innovation leadership over the past century by attracting global scientific talent, China has increasingly sought to replicate this logic through recruitment programs, returnee entrepreneurship, research funding, and institutional support.Many China-born scientists, executives, and entrepreneurs in biotechnology received their education or professional training in the United States before returning home. In doing so, they carried not only technical expertise but also tacit knowledge of how the U.S. innovation system functions—from laboratory management and venture formation to clinical development and regulatory strategy.This channel is now being reinforced by shifting patterns of global mobility. A growing number of internationally trained researchers are choosing to return to China or pursue opportunities elsewhere as uncertainty increases around research funding, immigration policy, and the broader academic environment in the United States. To the extent this trend continues, it could strengthen China’s innovation ecosystem while gradually weakening one of America’s longstanding advantages.

Capital Formation and Industrial Policy

Capital formation has become another important mechanism of convergence. China’s growing emphasis on combining state-backed capital with private investment has enabled rapid scaling, representing, in some ways, a rebalancing toward a more U.S.-style capital market model. Innovations such as government guidance funds complement earlier industrial policy approaches by channeling substantial resources into biotechnology and supporting long-term development.Rather than relying exclusively on state planning or market forces, China has increasingly sought to combine elements of both. This hybrid model has accelerated company formation, supported longer investment horizons, and enabled biotechnology firms to advance through development stages that might otherwise struggle to attract financing.

Global Integration and Technology Absorption

China has also integrated itself more deeply into global biomedical networks. Cross-border licensing deals, research partnerships, joint ventures, and other collaborative arrangements have facilitated capability building and accelerated the development of China’s biotechnology sector.In many cases, Chinese firms have learned not through isolation but through participation in global markets increasingly shaped by U.S. scientific leadership. Cross-border licensing agreements, in particular, have enabled Chinese firms to gain access to global development expertise and commercialization channels while allowing multinational pharmaceutical companies to access increasingly competitive Chinese innovation.At the same time, U.S. national security officials have raised concerns about forced technology transfer, intellectual property theft, and industrial espionage in parts of the biotechnology sector. Both dynamics can be true simultaneously: China has benefited substantially from legitimate participation in global innovation networks while also generating legitimate security concerns in certain areas.

Data and the AI-Biology Nexus

Data represents another emerging source of convergence. Although China maintains strict controls on human genetic resources and patient information, it has also worked to organize and modernize what was once a fragmented healthcare data environment. In selected domains, large-scale patient datasets have become meaningful innovation assets.Combined with China’s population scale, growing digital health infrastructure, and expanding AI capabilities, these data resources have the potential to accelerate biomedical research, clinical development, and healthcare delivery.At the same time, American policymakers increasingly argue that China’s approach remains fundamentally non-reciprocal—benefiting from access to foreign data and global scientific networks while maintaining substantial restrictions on access to its own data resources. This tension is likely to become more important as biotechnology and artificial intelligence become increasingly intertwined.

Regulatory and Legal Adaptation

Convergence has also occurred through regulatory and legal reform. Over the past decade, China has systematically modernized important parts of its intellectual property and pharmaceutical regulatory framework.The 2021 Patent Law introduced patent linkage mechanisms and patent term extensions that bear similarities to provisions in the U.S. Hatch-Waxman framework. More recently, China established formal drug trial data protection provisions that provide data exclusivity for innovative medicines. These changes do not eliminate longstanding concerns regarding intellectual property protection or enforcement, but they do reflect a broader effort to create a more innovation-oriented legal environment capable of supporting domestic biotechnology development.

Why Convergence Is Durable

What distinguishes these developments from a temporary competitive surge is that they are not isolated technological capabilities that can be easily constrained or reversed through a single policy intervention. Rather, they are embedded institutional capacities built through years of learning, adaptation, and integration into global scientific, financial, regulatory, and commercial systems. China has now moved well beyond simple manufacturing or technology absorption and has constructed substantial capabilities across multiple layers of the biotechnology value chain. Its progress may evolve unevenly across sectors, but it is unlikely to be reversed through policies framed around a fixed competitive window.For the United States, the implications are clear. Competing effectively will require more than reactive restrictions alone. It will require an affirmative, layer-specific strategy that recognizes where China is strong, where it remains weak, where U.S. advantages remain decisive, and where domestic capacity must be renewed. The objective should be to protect genuinely sensitive capabilities without undermining the openness, talent base, capital formation, regulatory credibility, and translational ecosystem that have long made the United States the global leader in biomedical innovation.

IV. Competing Smart: A Layer-Specific Approach

The structural nature of this convergence has direct implications for how both U.S. industry and policymakers should respond. The NSCEB report correctly identifies the urgency of a U.S. policy response. However, because its content sits within a larger competitive policy debate, its strategic warning can obscure the differentiated U.S. and Chinese strengths that should guide policy design. If China’s advances have been driven in significant part by copying, importing, transferring, and adopting the U.S. system, the more productive question is how this competition can act as a forcing mechanism, exposing structural inefficiencies in the U.S. system and accelerating long-overdue reforms, while protecting key advantages and capabilities. In short, effective U.S. strategy cannot simply be aimed at the futile prospect of reversing what China has achieved in biotechnology over decades—but rather must be focused on bolstering domestic U.S. competitiveness. The U.S. must run faster to win the race—not merely aim to slow down its competitor. It must promote as well as protect. And here, ironically, there are lessons that the U.S. can draw from China’s recent prowess.China’s policy approach—particularly in clinical trials, financing, and commercialization—has effectively compressed timelines, reduced costs, and aligned incentives across the innovation chain. These advantages are not the result of scale or top-down control per se, but of deliberate policy design choices that the United States can adapt without replicating China’s political model. Four areas stand out.

Regulatory Reform and Innovation Efficiency

First, regulatory reform. The U.S. system remains burdened by complex and often fragmented processes that increase uncertainty and delay time-to-market. By contrast, China's introduction of implied approval for clinical trials and expanded use of investigator-initiated trials has significantly accelerated early-stage development. This does not mean lowering standards, but it does suggest the need for reforms that streamline innovation pathways and reduce unnecessary friction in translating scientific discovery into clinical application. Fortunately, there is bipartisan support in Congress for FDA reform as a means of ensuring U.S. biomedical leadership. In February 2026, Senator Bill Cassidy, Chairman of the Senate Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions (HELP) Committee, published a landmark report outlining regulatory reform and proposals for modernizing the FDA to “maintain American biomedical dominance and ensure patients have timely access to the latest lifesaving treatments.” 

Financing Innovation and Bridging the Valley of Death

Second, the United States must do more to de-risk private capital in biotech. Biotech development is long, expensive, and risky—with a successful therapeutic requiring, on average, a decade and well over $1 billion to bring to market and having only a one-in-ten chance of success. While venture capital is essential, private markets alone may still underinvest in strategically important areas without policy support. China’s expanded use of government-guided funds has helped reduce downside risk while crowding in private capital. Although the U.S. system is fundamentally market-driven, it can still adopt policy catalysts—such as targeted funding mechanisms and public-private partnerships—that sustain and supercharge the innovation “flywheel” linking breakthrough therapies to future research and startup formation and thereby bridging the “valley of death.” 

Commercialization, Manufacturing, and Regional Innovation Clusters

Third, the United States faces structural weaknesses in pockets of its innovation ecosystem, particularly in the transition from discovery to commercialization. As the NSCEB report notes, limited pre-commercial-scale infrastructure and fragmented supply chains can slow the movement of new technologies from lab to market. China has addressed this challenge by, for one, co-locating research institutions, pilot-scale facilities, and manufacturing capacity within integrated regional clusters. There is a strong case for strengthening regional hubs in the United States that better connect research, development, and production, particularly in emerging fields such as biomanufacturing. This would also offer a politically durable model for place-based innovation. The 2025 Biomanufacturing Excellence Act, introduced in November 2025, would, for example, create a public-private National Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Center of Excellence focused on “advancing innovation in biopharmaceutical manufacturing methods.” Its bipartisan sponsorship by lawmakers from states such as Delaware, Pennsylvania, Indiana, and North Carolina underscores the political appeal of linking biotechnology competitiveness to regional manufacturing strength, skilled jobs, and supply-chain resilience.

Targeted Protection Rather Than Broad Disengagement

Fourth, policymakers must address legitimate concerns around intellectual property protection and non-market practices. The NSCEB report highlights risks such as IP leakage and theft, copycat activity, and pricing strategies that exploit structural weaknesses in global markets. These concerns warrant targeted responses, including enhanced monitoring, clearer regulatory safeguards, and, where necessary, enforcement actions. However, while these concerns are real and significant, policy responses must be calibrated in precise and nuanced ways to address and rectify real problems while avoiding overly simplistic and broadly generalizable solutions of disengagement, which could potentially reduce visibility into China’s system and weaken the United States’ own innovation ecosystem, without increasing U.S. competitiveness.At the same time, broad and indiscriminate efforts to pursue non-specific decoupling—without careful attention to specific policy challenges, national security concerns, and tradeoffs involved in preserving medical and health benefits—can be risky if not properly calibrated. Nor are overly reductionistic containment measures aimed at broadly severing ties across the entire value chain presented by China an adequate response given the multiple layers of this competition. As Corvino and Fluegel’s analysis of WuXi, the dominant contract manufacturing organization and biomanufacturing service-provider, illustrates, China’s advantages in biotech stem not from control over any single technological chokepoint but from deep integration across the drug development stack, making the ecosystem fundamentally resistant to the export-control logic that has shaped U.S. policy in semiconductors. In several domains, disengagement carries costs that outweigh its benefits—reducing visibility, ceding standard-setting influence, or simply leaving space for others to fill. The more effective posture, in some layers, is active standard-setting, calibrated access, and smart competition from a position of strength.

The Limits of Containment

The Biden administration’s January 2025 controls on high-parameter flow cytometers and certain mass spectrometry equipment underscore genuine dual-use concerns, but they also illustrate why biotech cannot be managed through semiconductor-style chokepoint controls alone—given that unlike compute as an underlying factor in the AI race, there is no single piece of hardware upon which the entire biotech industry is built. The debate over the BIOSECURE Act underscores this tension: reducing reliance on Chinese biomanufacturing service providers may address real security concerns that stem from dependence on China, but doing so without calibrating the response to account for viable U.S. alternatives and presenting an affirmative vision of competition that relies on U.S. strength could also raise costs and disrupt development timelines for some U.S. biotech firms, thereby hampering U.S. biotech innovation in the short term. In short, effective policy to compete in biotech cannot be merely “anti-China” and defensive but rather must be “pro-America” and one that harnesses and promotes U.S. strength.

Layer One: Basic Science and Research Openness

At the level of fundamental science, openness remains not only desirable but necessary if the United States seeks to sustain the scientific enterprise that has enabled its global research leadership over the past century. Scientific discovery is inherently cumulative and global, built on shared knowledge, cross-border collaboration, and open publication, particularly in basic research that aims to uncover novel insights about life and living systems. Patterns of co-authorship and shared research infrastructure highlight the extent to which modern science operates as a transnational system. Preserving openness in basic research and pre-competitive collaboration is a strategic imperative. The United States’ advantage in this domain stems from its ability to attract global talent and function as a central node in the global knowledge network. This advantage would be undermined if openness were eroded, though there are serious concerns around research security that warrant careful attention and calibration.

Layer Two: Clinical Development and Trial Infrastructure

At the level of clinical trial capabilities, where China’s advantages are structural, it is important to recognize that despite U.S. policies, European and Japanese firms, among others, would continue to partner with China, allowing the benefits of its clinical infrastructure. Cutting China off unilaterally will simply mean U.S. firms losing access to a critical component of the innovation pipeline. A more effective policy stance would involve participating in China-linked clinical ecosystems while shaping standards, ensuring data integrity, and preserving access to resulting innovation. Japanese pharmaceutical companies are already seeking China-linked discovery partnerships, and regulators in markets such as the United Kingdom and the European Union have debated wider acceptance of China-origin clinical trial data even as the U.S. FDA has remained more cautious toward later-stage China-only evidence. Moreover, the United States must reform its regulatory regime to compete more effectively.

Layer Three: Data Governance and Global Standards

In the domain of data governance, competition is both inevitable and necessary. Biotech is increasingly data-driven, especially with advances in genomics and AI-enabled drug discovery reshaping the industry. The central issues here are data quality, security, and interoperability. The United States retains a first-mover advantage in setting global standards, particularly in areas such as transparency and ethical governance. The objective should be to define the global rules under which data operate. This includes developing frameworks such as federated biotech data systems that allow analysis without centralized data transfer, as well as international standards that ensure accountability and security. Engagement with China in this domain—however cautious and clear-eyed—may be helpful to ensure that global standards reflect U.S. principles rather than emerging in parallel systems shaped by others.

Layer Four: Supply Chains, APIs, and Industrial Resilience

The final layer concerns supply chains and APIs, and uniquely, this is the one layer where reducing dependence is not self-defeating. The concern is real: China’s position in APIs and upstream pharmaceutical inputs can create chokepoint leverage, akin to rare earths and critical mineral dependencies. But the problem is often mischaracterized: U.S. dependence on China here reflects differences in cost structure and industrial organization. Domestic capacity can be rebuilt through targeted interventions—subsidies, procurement guarantees, and regulatory support—at a scale of investment that is modest relative to other industrial policy efforts. This makes API reshoring not only feasible, but one of the more viable components of a broader biotechnology strategy. There is no element of API manufacturing and production of biologics that physically is controlled by China and could not be reshored or diversified. APIs are not mineral ores, located only within specific geographical borders. Rather, the United States can—and should—invest in industries and industrial processes like fine chemistry, which was once a strong domestic capability, while also ensuring diversification of supply chains.

India as a Strategic Partner

India deserves explicit attention in this layer. It is not a direct substitute for China’s emerging innovation capabilities—and indeed, many upstream starting materials that go into India’s API output are sourced from China—but it is a major pharmaceutical manufacturer and an indispensable part of the global generics, APIs, and vaccine supply chain. Any serious U.S. strategy for reducing overdependence on China should therefore consider the role of India and other trusted manufacturing partners as part of a diversified resilience strategy rather than framing the supply-chain challenge as a binary U.S.-China problem.

Recent Legislative Responses

Recent congressional proposals, including the Biotechnology Innovation and National Security Act (BINSA), the COINS Act, and related legislation, reflect growing concern in Washington over the rapid expansion of licensing, investment, and partnership activity between U.S. pharmaceutical companies and Chinese biotechnology firms. Supporters of these measures argue that the trend risks accelerating China’s long-term biotechnology capabilities, increasing U.S. dependence on Chinese-sourced innovation, and reinforcing broader patterns of technological diffusion that could ultimately weaken America’s competitive position. These concerns are not without merit. The scale of recent licensing activity underscores China’s growing role in the global biopharma development ecosystem and raises legitimate questions about how economic integration intersects with long-term strategic competition. And indeed, given China’s advantage in industrial scale and cost, projecting current trends farther out would indeed suggest a weakened and less competitive U.S. biotech landscape.At the same time, such measures should be understood as only one component of a broader competitiveness strategy rather than a standalone solution. Restrictions on licensing, investment, or commercial partnerships may reduce specific risks and address particular dependencies, but they do not by themselves inherently strengthen the underlying drivers of U.S. biotechnology leadership. The long-term competitive balance will ultimately depend on the vitality of the U.S. innovation ecosystem—including basic research, clinical development, regulatory efficiency, talent attraction, manufacturing capacity, and access to capital. In this sense, defensive measures designed to manage risk are most likely to be effective when paired with affirmative policies that expand U.S. capabilities and make the United States the preferred location for biotechnology innovation, development, and commercialization. To invoke a line from renowned Stanford synthetic biologist Drew Endy, who opined on the China biotech rivalry, the United States must win by winning.

A Four-Part Strategy: Protect, Promote, Partner, and Shape

Whether or not the above legislative proposals constitute a coherent U.S. strategy, an effective approach should be organized around four complementary objectives: protecting, promoting, partnering, and shaping. Protection is necessary in areas tied directly to national security, including sensitive data, critical supply chains, and certain dual-use technologies. Promotion requires strengthening domestic capabilities, from basic research funding to biomanufacturing infrastructure and public-private investment. Partnership remains possible only in narrow domains where bounded cooperation advances U.S. interests and patient outcomes. Shaping refers to the United States’ role in setting global standards for data, regulation, ethics, and safety.The United States should be confident that it remains in a position of strength, but that position is not intrinsic, inevitable, or self-sustaining. Maintaining leadership will require not only responding to China’s rise but also addressing domestic constraints and leveraging global interdependence more effectively.In this sense, competition with China should not be viewed solely as a threat. It is also a catalyst—one that can push the United States to modernize its own biotech system, refine its policy tools, and reassert leadership by shaping it more deliberately.

V. Beyond Competition: Why Health Is Different

As consequential as this competition is, and as urgent as the need for the U.S. to safeguard its economic and national security in biotech may be, it is not the whole story. U.S.-China biotech competition needs to be right-sized: biotechnology is strategically important, but it should not be reduced solely to another arena of great-power rivalry. The asymmetric nature of each country’s capabilities, the deeply interdependent structure of modern biomedical science, and the sector’s most fundamental attribute—that its stakes, impact, and direct downstream benefits are measured in human lives and the alleviation of human disease and suffering (which make biotechnology distinct from other general purpose technologies)—together make selective, targeted, and de-risked cooperation in the broader health domain one of the most important, commercially critical, and politically viable stabilizers remaining in the U.S.-China relationship.Ultimately, the U.S. biotech agenda should not only be fixated intensely on competing but also embrace a more human-centered framework—one focused not only on competition in science and innovation, but also on the larger purpose they should serve: curing disease, extending health span, and improving human wellbeing. At a time when trust between the United States and China is in short supply across technology, security, trade, and governance, health and medicine remain a rare domain characterized by shared vulnerabilities and overlapping national interests. Cancer, aging populations, mental health, chronic disease burdens, pandemic preparedness, antimicrobial resistance, and biotechnology governance are systemic challenges neither country can solve alone.The recent Trump-Xi summit readouts are notable in this regard. The Chinese side explicitly listed “health” immediately after “economy and trade” as an area for expanded cooperation—an ordering likely intentional. This suggests Beijing views health as one of the few areas where meaningful cooperation with Washington remains possible despite broader strategic competition. There is also a U.S. basis for limited cooperation: the renewed U.S.-China Science and Technology Agreement, more narrowly focused on basic research and additional safeguards, preserves a workable framework for carefully bounded scientific engagement.

Why Health Is Different

More specifically, selective and de-risked cooperation in medicine possesses several characteristics that make it uniquely resilient within the broader bilateral relationship.First, it is politically less zero-sum and sensitive than many other sectors. Cooperation can produce visible domestic benefits for both societies simultaneously—and prove intuitively resonant to the American public. Unlike semiconductors or military technologies, breakthroughs in cancer treatment, prevention, and public health infrastructure do not necessarily require one side’s gain to come at the other’s expense. Second, health creates practical incentives against full decoupling even amid strategic rivalry. The United States still relies heavily on China for parts of the pharmaceutical supply chain and clinical scale capacity, while China continues to depend on the United States for frontier biomedical research, innovation ecosystems, regulatory approval pathways, and capital access. While ensuring that the U.S. reduces its strategic dependencies that could pose national security threats is important, doing so will require calibration and diversification, undergirded by U.S. capacity, rather than blanket bifurcation, since completely severing all ties without U.S. alternatives would impose substantial costs on both sides in the short run.Third, health can serve as a form of strategic risk management. Even during periods of geopolitical tension, sustained ties between scientists, doctors, medical professionals, hospitals, universities, and regulators can help preserve institutional communication channels. Historically, scientific and medical cooperation have often functioned as stabilizing ballast during periods of political volatility. During the Cold War, for example, the United States, while denouncing the Soviet Union as an evil empire with an expansionist ideology that it was committed to relegating to the ash heap of history, still pursued smallpox eradication jointly, arguing that to do so was a common victory for humanity and did not undermine either country’s national security interests. Fourth, biotechnology’s rapid evolution is engendering novel risks and ethical challenges and therefore emerging as a domain of shared responsibility. As China advances from fast follower to genuine innovator, both countries now bear growing responsibility for setting the standards—around biosecurity, clinical governance, AI-enabled biology, and data governance—that will shape the field globally.

The Real Question

The real question, therefore, is not whether the United States and China should compete in biotechnology—structural realities ensure that they already do, and intensively—but rather where national security concerns justify decoupling in ways that advance U.S. interests without imposing self-defeating costs, and, more importantly, where, how, and under what safeguards limited collaboration may still serve U.S. interests.

VI. A Practical Agenda for Selective Collaboration

Because much of the U.S. policy debate is understandably focused on competition, this paper proposes a practical agenda for answering a different but equally important question: where, how, and under what safeguards might selective collaboration advance U.S. interests while preserving national security?The objective is not broad engagement for its own sake. Nor is it a return to the assumptions that characterized earlier periods of U.S.-China scientific cooperation. Rather, the goal is to identify narrow areas where collaboration can deliver tangible benefits to American patients, reduce shared risks, and preserve channels of professional exchange without creating strategic vulnerability.The agenda is organized around three objectives: advancing patient outcomes, managing shared risks, and preserving innovation while avoiding dependency. Within each category, cooperation should be narrowly scoped, made reciprocal where possible, and subject to clear safeguards.

1. Build Disease-Specific Clinical Data Corridors, Starting with Cancer

Advancing Patient OutcomesThe United States and China should pursue clinical data harmonization as a narrowly scoped but high-impact area of cooperation, beginning with cancer as a pilot sector. The objective would be to align clinical trial standards, data protocols, endpoints, and real-world evidence frameworks in ways that improve interoperability, while preserving privacy, biosecurity, intellectual property rights, and national security interests through appropriate safeguards.Clinical trials remain among the most time-consuming and expensive stages of drug development. China has emerged as a leading destination for clinical research due to its large patient base, rapid recruitment capacity, and increasingly efficient approval processes. As trials become more globally distributed, however, differences in regulatory standards and data-sharing rules are becoming a growing bottleneck.The potential benefits are substantial. A recent Harvard Business Review analysis of approval delays for pembrolizumab and enzalutamide across high-cancer-burden countries estimated that greater international standardization of clinical trial and regulatory requirements could enable more concurrent approvals of innovative oncology therapies, potentially reducing global cancer mortality by 10–20% and saving on the order of one to two million lives annually.The largest barrier to U.S.-China collaboration remains differences in data-sharing regulations, particularly those related to China’s human genetic resources (HGR) regime. Recent reforms, including the transfer of oversight from the Ministry of Science and Technology to the National Health Commission and proposals to raise thresholds for international HGR collection, suggest some movement toward greater openness. Oncology—particularly lung cancer, rare cancers, and other high-burden malignancies—offers a promising starting point because of its large patient populations, urgent unmet needs, and clear benefits for patients in both countries. The goal should not be unrestricted data sharing, but the development of interoperable standards that enable meaningful collaboration under carefully defined safeguards.

2.⁠ ⁠Create Rare Disease Partnerships Where Chinese Patient Cohorts Can Help U.S. Patients

Relatedly, another compelling area for bounded cooperation lies in rare disease or rare cancer research, particularly in cases where U.S. patient populations are too small to support timely clinical studies on their own. China’s large population means that even diseases considered ultra-rare in the United States may still have sufficiently large patient cohorts in China to support meaningful research and trial recruitment.This dynamic is especially relevant for certain inherited blood disorders such as thalassemia, which has significantly higher prevalence in parts of southern China but is rare in the United States. A study estimated that carrier rates for α-thalassemia and β-thalassemia are as high as 14.13% and 4.91%, respectively, in some southern regions of China. In such cases, China’s large patient population could meaningfully support U.S. efforts in patient recruitment and clinical evidence generation for rare disease research.Even for ultra-rare diseases where no single country can easily assemble sufficiently large cohorts for timely evidence generation, China’s large population base and rapidly expanding biobanking infrastructure provide an important complementary resource for international collaboration. China’s National GeneBank (CNGB) is among the largest and most advanced biobanking infrastructures globally, supported by substantial investment in large-scale sample collection and data integration. At the same time, China’s biobanking system still faces challenges in areas such as standardization, data interoperability, and methodological best practices, making international collaboration valuable for improving the global usability and comparability of Chinese biomedical data. Of course, such potential partnerships will depend on China’s willingness to share data with greater transparency than it has done in the past. But if both sides perceive clear benefits and the cooperation can be structured in ways that insulate it from any national security concerns, this area may offer meaningful opportunities for collaboration.For rare diseases, the moral case for collaboration is perhaps the most direct: in some cases, without access to Chinese patient cohorts, American patients could simply be left waiting and dying. This is where engagement with China is not a concession but squarely in U.S. interests as a direct benefit to American patients.

3.⁠ ⁠Treat Mental Health as a Shared Public Health Challenge

Mental health represents an underexplored area of U.S.-China health cooperation. Both countries face a rising burden of mental health disorders, including depression and anxiety, alongside persistent gaps in diagnosis, treatment access, stigma reduction, and workforce capacity. China is estimated to have more than 173 million adults affected by one or more mental disorders, while about 23.4% of U.S. adults—roughly 61.5 million people—experienced mental health challenges. These challenges are driven less by differences in disease profiles than by systemic constraints in care delivery and service availability.This makes mental health a comparatively low-security but high-impact field for cooperation. Unlike frontier biotechnology, this is a domain of asymmetric need: China’s mental health system remains heavily hospital-centered and oriented toward severe psychiatric illness, while community-based and outpatient care are still underdeveloped. Collaboration could focus on where the U.S. has more mature experience, including scalable care models, digital mental health tools, workforce development, AI-enabled screening with appropriate safeguards, school- and workplace-based interventions, and clinician training. It also helps broaden the bilateral health agenda beyond elite biotechnology and drug discovery toward population-level public health challenges.

4.⁠ Make Biosecurity and Biosafety Guardrails a Prerequisite for Collaboration

Managing Shared RisksAny proposal for limited collaboration will inevitably encounter concerns over China’s dual-use research, data security, laboratory safety, military-civil fusion, and supply-chain vulnerabilities, as well as broader challenges of trust and verification. Rather than treating these concerns as insurmountable barriers that preclude dialogue, they should be understood as the necessary conditions and constraints within which any engagement must operate. This may include discussions on biomedical research governance, including laboratory biosafety, clinical trial transparency, and AI-bio risk assessment, as well as the development of institutional standard-setting bodies, and agreements on transparency and disclosure. The United States should be realistic about the political obstacles to engagement, clear-eyed about China’s history of dissembling, start from small initial steps, and insist on demonstrated and verifiable transparency—without taking any of China’s commitments for granted. National security concerns should be treated as non-negotiable principles, and engagement should never come at the expense of security. At the same time, the emergence of increasingly disruptive biotechnologies that pose significant ethical, biosafety, and other risks to human health should provide a reason to pursue engagement when doing so clearly serves U.S. interests. 

5. Establish Risk-Reduction Dialogue on AI-Bio Convergence

The urgency of risk reduction has grown significantly as biotechnology converges with artificial intelligence. Recent research has shown that AI-powered protein design tools can generate modified DNA sequences for hazardous proteins that may evade existing screening systems used by DNA synthesis companies, creating a credible pathway for misuse by rogue actors, terrorist groups, non-state organizations, or malicious insiders. These are risks that neither the United States nor China has an interest in enabling.One practical area for engagement therefore involves strengthening DNA synthesis screening standards. Given that China receives more than 30% of global DNA synthesis orders, effective safeguards require meaningful implementation on both sides. While the United States has begun responding through executive actions and gene-synthesis oversight initiatives, implementation remains uneven internationally. At present, researchers may be able to engage in regulatory arbitrage by ordering synthetic DNA from providers operating under weaker screening requirements. Governments and regulators should therefore explore stronger screening requirements, updated databases of hazardous sequences and emerging variants, and compatible governance frameworks that keep pace with technological change.The challenge, however, extends beyond DNA synthesis alone. Frontier biological foundation models and increasingly capable agentic AI systems are lowering barriers to biological design and experimentation. Open-source biological models have already demonstrated the ability to generate novel viral genomes and assist increasingly sophisticated biological design tasks. As these capabilities advance, both countries have an interest in discussing appropriate safeguards, including access controls, risk monitoring, coordination between AI developers and biosecurity authorities, and standards for responsible deployment of biological design tools.The emergence of agentic AI adds a further layer of risk. Unlike earlier AI systems that primarily generate information, agentic systems can autonomously execute tasks, use tools, coordinate workflows, and make iterative decisions with limited human supervision. As these capabilities are integrated into biological research environments, they could dramatically lower barriers to biological experimentation and amplify the scale, speed, and accessibility of potentially dangerous activities. As a result, both countries have an interest in discussing safeguards for advanced biological AI systems, including access controls, risk monitoring, human oversight requirements, and coordination between AI developers and biosecurity authorities.Such dialogue should not be viewed as a substitute for deterrence, surveillance, export controls, or other national security measures. Rather, it should be understood as a complementary effort to reduce risks from accidents, misuse, and non-state actors. While cooperation will not eliminate strategic distrust, biosecurity may be one of the few domains where both countries share a direct interest in preventing failure. Given the potentially global consequences of biological accidents or misuse, even limited progress could yield outsized benefits.

6.⁠ ⁠ Explore Opportunities to Improve Affordability without Creating Dependency

Preserving Innovation While Avoiding DependencyAddressing exorbitant healthcare costs is another important area that could theoretically benefit from U.S.-China engagement, particularly by improving both the affordability of pharmaceuticals and the cost of medical services.For U.S. drugmakers, one major pressure point is the looming patent cliff. More than $200 billion in annual pharmaceutical revenue is estimated to face loss of exclusivity through 2030, as blockbuster drugs, including major GLP-1 therapies, increasingly encounter generic and biosimilar competition. This creates mounting pressure on large pharmaceutical firms to reduce costs while sustaining innovation pipelines. China’s cost advantages could help alleviate some of these pressures by supporting more cost-efficient drug development and production while expanding therapeutic options by leveraging China as an R&D hub. U.S. healthcare delivery is also facing severe affordability pressure. U.S. healthcare services are extraordinarily expensive, with nearly half of American adults reporting difficulty affording medical care. At the same time, the risk is that by overemphasizing and benefiting from China’s economic advantages, the U.S. pharmaceutical industry grows increasingly reliant on Chinese assets, propping up China’s biotech sector with ample capital, while early-stage U.S. biotechnology firms face competitive disadvantages that result in the long-term weakening of U.S. competitiveness. These are serious tradeoffs that must be accounted for. Therefore, a key precondition of cost reductions through China engagement must be that the U.S. biopharma industry does not, in doing so, create or exacerbate dependencies—and must not do so at the expense of U.S. innovation and competitiveness. Rather, targeted ways to leverage efficiencies in the Chinese ecosystem—in short to do more with less—without sacrificing resilience, innovation, and domestic strength will be the objective, which will require nuanced strategies that account for tradeoffs.To be sure, the United States should not aim to pursue a naïve conception of broad healthcare integration, but rather recognize that selective collaboration on generics, biosimilars, diagnostics, manufacturing, and certain medical services could help relieve affordability pressures while preserving quality controls and regulatory safeguards. Of course, the challenge will be to evaluate tradeoffs, particularly between short-term advantages in economics against the possibility of developing long-term strategic dependencies that are not in U.S. interests. 

7.⁠ ⁠Invest in Talent Co-Development and Professional Exchange

Another area for selective cooperation lies in professional training and institutional exchange, particularly in clinical trial design, regulatory science, bioethics, pharmacovigilance, hospital administration, and public health implementation. Such exchanges should be guided by two principles. First, they should be genuinely reciprocal rather than one-way transfers of knowledge. China’s large patient population and growing strengths in clinical development mean that both sides have practical experience and best practices to share. Second, cooperation should focus on management, regulatory, and implementation domains rather than sensitive scientific research or advanced technologies.The logic is straightforward: the United States and China possess different strengths across the biomedical ecosystem. Many of these capabilities—including China’s clinical recruitment expertise and America’s translational research and regulatory experience—are difficult to transfer through written guidance alone and instead depend on institutional learning and professional exchange.At the same time, the United States should not lose sight of a broader lesson. American scientific leadership has long benefited from its ability to attract, integrate, and retain global talent, including generations of Chinese scientists, researchers, and entrepreneurs. Maintaining that advantage will require sustained investment in talent retention and a research environment that remains attractive to the world’s best minds.Properly structured exchanges need not weaken U.S. competitiveness. Instead, they can strengthen healthcare and regulatory capacity, improve implementation, and preserve channels of professional dialogue even amid broader geopolitical competition. The renewed U.S.-China Science and Technology Agreement, with its narrower scope and stronger safeguards, provides one possible framework for carefully bounded engagement.Any such exchanges should include strict guardrails: clear subject-matter boundaries, participant screening, transparency around funding and affiliations, protection of proprietary information, and exclusion of sensitive technologies. The focus should remain on non-sensitive areas such as trial ethics, patient safety, adverse-event monitoring, hospital quality systems, regulatory evidence standards, and public health implementation. The objective is not to rebuild an unrestricted scientific commons, but to create narrowly tailored channels of practical cooperation where both countries have something to teach, something to learn, and where patients ultimately stand to benefit.

VII. Conclusion

This paper argues that the dominant frameworks shaping much of current discourse about biotech competition are not fully adequate for capturing the complexity of what is unfolding. Linear race narratives, analogies to other more discrete technological capabilities, fixed competitive windows, and reflexive calls for sweeping decoupling can obscure the real nature of the challenge. They also risk diverting attention from what the United States most needs: an affirmative strategy that competes from a position of strength, invests in domestic capacity, and carefully distinguishes among areas that require restriction, protection, resilience, competition, and selective cooperation subject to clear guardrails.Biotechnology is fundamentally different from semiconductors. Its capabilities are distributed unevenly across the drug development value chain, from basic discovery and translational science to clinical trials, manufacturing, regulatory approval, commercialization, and patient access. The United States and China occupy different positions within this system. Policies built on the assumption that export-control-style restrictions can simply halt China’s biotech progress risk misunderstanding the complex nature of the sector itself. Without a deliberate affirmative strategy to leverage U.S. strengths while investing in American capacity, broad and reactive measures may fail to slow China’s biotech ecosystem in targeted ways while imposing significant costs on the United States. More fundamentally, biotechnology is a sector in which the costs of poorly designed strategy are not merely political or economic. They can also be measured in human lives, health, and wellbeing. Cancer, rare diseases, mental health, chronic illness, aging populations, pandemic preparedness, antimicrobial resistance, and biotechnology governance are systemic challenges that do not stop at national borders. In this context, selective cooperation in carefully bounded areas is not a concession. It is a tool for advancing U.S. interests, preserving American visibility and influence, strengthening global standards, and accelerating progress against universal health threats.A purely restrictive posture also risks accelerating the very outcome U.S. policymakers should seek to avoid: a China-led global biopharma development ecosystem that increasingly operates outside U.S. influence. That parallel system is already taking shape, as China’s share of global clinical trials rises and European regulators and pharmaceutical firms show growing willingness to evaluate and commercialize Chinese-developed therapies. If Washington responds by rejecting China-linked biotechnology wholesale, it could push more drug development, regulatory learning, and innovation pathways outside the U.S. system. The result could soon be a politically untenable scenario: American cancer patients traveling to China or Europe to access lifesaving China-developed drugs unavailable at home.The proposals outlined in this paper therefore should not be misconstrued as a call for broad scientific openness or unrestricted collaboration. That era has long passed, and intense geopolitical competition, strategic distrust, and rivalry in a powerful technological domain will persist as an underlying structural feature. Instead, these proposals should be bounded by clear safeguards, focused on specific diseases and patient needs, and designed to preserve U.S. leverage within the global biomedical ecosystem. In a relationship increasingly defined by distrust, health will not—and should not—resolve the broader strategic rivalry between Washington and Beijing. But it may remain one of the few domains where the United States can compete vigorously, protect what matters, and still sustain carefully controlled channels that serve American interests and human health. In a field where the ultimate stakes are human lives, health, and well-being, preserving the balance between strategic competition and carefully bounded cooperation is both morally necessary and strategically wise.
